title: Water Resources & Mining Operations
description: This cluster situates water as the strategic resource in dispute, connecting hydroelectric tensions and indigenous rights in the central-south with the extractive pressure of lithium and copper in the north. It integrates historical conflicts (Ralco, Alto Maipo) with the new geopolitics of salt flats in Antofagasta and the insecurity of tailings in Coquimbo, where industrial reorganization redefines territorial relations. Altogether, it reveals how the energy transition operates as a reconfiguration of “hydrosocial territories,” inevitably intertwining mining-energy expansion with community life.

title: Environmental Impacts & Energy Projects
description: The controversies grouped in this cluster evidence how energy projects translate into socio-ecological disputes, articulating pressure on ecosystems and glaciers with the judicialization of conflicts in environmental courts. The emergence of green hydrogen in Magallanes stands out, oscillating between export promise and a new frontier of territorial friction facing protection demands. Thus, the transition manifests simultaneously as a physical transformation of the landscape and as institutional litigation over ecological protection standards.

title: Industrial Pollution & Decarbonization
description: The central tension of this cluster resides in the clash between the heritage of pollution in “sacrifice zones” (Quintero-Puchuncaví, Coronel) and the programmatic decarbonization agenda. There, the accumulated health crisis confronts the policy of shutting down thermoelectric plants, configuring a scenario where energy modernization collides with the persistence of environmental damage and labor resistance.

title: Environmental Institutions
description: Shifting focus from territory to governance, this cluster situates conflict within the regulatory infrastructure of the Environmental Assessment Service (SEA) and state legislative production. Here, institutional oversight capacity is debated against the need to negotiate normative frameworks that order a capital-intensive transformation. The dispute transcends opposition to specific projects to focus on the definition of the rules of the game themselves, which, inevitably, structure the energy transition.

title: Local Impacts & Communities
description: This cluster shows an approach to the local and everyday: here, the transition is experienced from the communities, where impacts that might seem secondary become public conflict. Temporally, this topic converges toward the conflict over light pollution that the construction of a Green Hydrogen project would generate over the Paranal astronomical observatory in Chile. Thus, this cluster shows how the energy transition also alters sensitive living conditions and communities, with the international scientific community being an actor with significant media advocacy capacity.
